items.q: Why does a lino print come out reversed?
items.correct: The block mirrors the design, so the printed image is flipped left-to-right.
items.misconception: The image prints exactly as drawn on the block.
items.why: Students forget the print is a mirror of the block, which matters especially when lettering prints backwards.

items.q: Is sculpture only made by carving?
items.correct: No — sculpture can also be modelled, cast or constructed, as well as carved.
items.misconception: Yes — real sculpture is always carved from stone or wood.
items.random_wrong: No — sculpture is any flat artwork with texture.
items.why: Students fixate on carving and overlook additive processes such as modelling clay and constructing assemblages.

items.q: How do you make a colour lighter in watercolour?
items.correct: Add more water so the white of the paper shows through the thinner wash.
items.misconception: Add white paint, as you would with acrylics or oils.
items.random_wrong: Add a small amount of black paint.
items.why: Students transfer the acrylic habit of adding white, but transparent watercolour relies on dilution and the paper's whiteness.

items.q: Which fine art process produces multiple copies of one image?
items.why: Correct answer: Printmaking. “Impasto painting” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Carving is called a subtractive sculpture process because:
items.correct: Material is removed to reveal the form.
items.random_wrong: Metal is poured into a mould.
items.why: It's Material is removed to reveal the form.. “Material is added in layers.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Paint applied so thickly that marks stand off the surface.
items.why: Correct answer: Paint applied so thickly that marks stand off the surface.. “A type of printing ink.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Lino printing belongs to which family of printmaking?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Relief. “Stencil” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A lino print edition is numbered up to 12/12. How many prints are in the edition?
items.why: Exact answer: 12 prints. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
